evals.prompt: How should I type my React component that accepts children and a className prop?
evals.expected_output: Use interface Props with PropsWithChildren<T> or explicit React.ReactNode: interface Props { className?: string; children: React.ReactNode; }. Don't use React.FC — it has deprecated implicit children. Return type: JSX.Element or ReactNode. Use ComponentPropsWithoutRef<'div'> to extend native div attributes safely.

evals.prompt: Type a generic list component that works with any data type and renders each item.
evals.expected_output:
Use generic component: interface ListProps { items: T[]; renderItem: (item: T) => ReactNode; keyExtractor: (item: T) => string; }. Component signature: function List(props: ListProps): JSX.Element. Comma after T prevents TSX parser confusion. Use in: items={users} renderItem={(u) =>
{u.name}
} keyExtractor={(u) => u.id} />

evals.prompt: I'm using React.FC to type all my components. Is this a problem?
evals.expected_output: Yes — React.FC is problematic. Violation: No React.FC. It includes implicit children (deprecated), has return type issues, and is verbose. Fix: use plain function declarations with typed Props interface. Before: const Component: React.FC<Props> = (props) => {}. After: function Component(props: Props): JSX.Element {}. Explicitly type children as React.ReactNode if needed.

should_not_trigger: Configure a PostgreSQL connection pool in Go
Write a shell script for database backup
Set up Terraform for AWS infrastructure
